# Kevin Warsh sidesteps questions over Donald Trump’s influence on Fed rate policy

Nominee to head US central bank grilled by senators over president’s public calls for cuts

![](https://images.ft.com/v3/image/raw/https%3A%2F%2Fd1e00ek4ebabms.cloudfront.net%2Fproduction%2F8b73e268-a44f-4bf1-95f1-317cf290e386.jpg?source=next-article&fit=scale-down&quality=highest&width=700&dpr=1)Kevin Warsh: ‘The president never asked me to predetermine, commit, fix or decide on any interest rate decision, in any of our discussions’© Bloomberg

Kevin Warsh sidestepped accusations that he would succumb to pressure from Donald Trump to cut interest rates, as the president’s pick to lead the US Federal Reserve was grilled by senators over whether he would remain independent of the White House.

In a confident performance before the influential Senate banking committee on Tuesday, [Warsh](https://www.ft.com/stream/005e7e07-099d-4671-b5d6-6958fbe73520 "") denied that Trump had asked him to reduce interest rates during the interview process.

“The president never asked me to predetermine, commit, fix or decide on any interest rate decision, in any of our discussions,” the Fed nominee said. “Nor would I ever agree to do so.”

Warsh’s confirmation hearing comes at a time of heightened concern over the Fed’s independence to set interest rates free from interference from the executive branch amid frequent calls from Trump for the central bank to slash US borrowing costs.

The Trump administration has launched a criminal investigation into current [Fed](https://www.ft.com/federal-reserve "") chair Jay Powell over his handling of cost overruns for the central bank’s $2.5bn refurbishment of its headquarters, which he says is an attempt to pressure officials into making rate cuts.

The president said ahead of the hearing that the US should have the lowest interest rates in the world — namechecking the Swiss central bank, which has a benchmark interest rate of zero. The Fed’s benchmark range is between 3.5 and 3.75 per cent.

The president has recently threatened to fire Powell should he remain at the central bank beyond the official end of his second term on May 15. Trump also attempted to sack Fed governor Lisa Cook last summer in a case now before the Supreme Court.

Warsh said Trump’s calls for aggressive rate cuts were in line with historical norms: “I’ve heard his view on interest rates and it sounded very similar to me to every other president in economic history.”

But in one uncomfortable exchange that laid bare the political pressures he is likely to face as Trump’s pick to lead the Fed, Warsh declined to answer Elizabeth Warren, the highest-ranking Democrat on the committee, when she asked whether he thought the president had lost the 2020 election — which Trump claims, without evidence, to have won. “We try to keep politics out of the Fed,” Warsh said.

The hearing was just one hurdle Warsh will need to clear if he is to be confirmed as Powell’s replacement.

Tim Scott, the Republican who chairs the banking committee, has yet to set a date for the panel to vote on his nomination.

Warsh will then need to be rubber-stamped by a majority of the 24 senators on the committee before his nomination can be taken up by the full 100-member Senate.

But that is unlikely to happen if the probe of Powell continues, with North Carolina Republican Thom Tillis insisting that he will block the nomination from advancing out of the committee stage until the investigation is dropped.

Tillis railed against the Department of Justice probe at the committee hearing, saying: “If we put everybody in prison in federal government that had had a budget go over, we’d have to reserve an area roughly the size of Texas for a penal colony.”

Powell has said he believes he has the right to remain at the helm of the Fed if Warsh is not confirmed in the Senate when his term expires, setting up another legal battle should Trump carry out his threat to fire him.

Warsh, nominated by Trump earlier this year, is estimated to hold at least $130mn in assets, many of which he listed as confidential on financial disclosures he filed last week.

Warren on Tuesday called on him to disclose when he planned to sell some of his “secret” assets and to confirm whether he had holdings in any companies affiliated with the president.

The nominee replied he had “come to full agreement” with the Office of Government Ethics on divesting the assets to meet government rules and was likely to invest the proceeds in cash and Treasury bonds.

Warsh, who previously served as a Fed governor, also promised that if confirmed he would “hit the ground running” in carrying out far-reaching reforms to the world’s leading central bank, focusing on shrinking its balance sheet and overhauling the way it engages with the public.

“My experience of the Fed, my understanding of the people, the culture, the governance and the things that are ripe for reform are going to give me a leg up,” he told senators. “What the Fed needs is reform to its frameworks and reforms to its communications.”

_Additional reporting by Amelia Pollard and Kate Duguid in New York_

UnitedHealth has increased its 2026 earnings guidance after it raised prices and reduced the number of patients it took on some government insurance schemes, as the US healthcare group seeks to steady itself.

Minneapolis-based UnitedHealth said on Tuesday that it expected adjusted net earnings to be at least $18.25 per share, up from the $17.75 it predicted in January.

The company‘s shares rose 6 per cent in pre-market trading, even as it reported a dip in first-quarter earnings to $9bn, from $9.1bn in the same period a year ago.

UnitedHealth’s guidance suggests that the company is recovering from a period of turbulence, which was partly sparked by the Trump administration’s proposal to throttle spending on some government-subsidised insurance plans.

UnitedHealth’s [stock plunged](https://www.ft.com/content/02bc222e-4a66-4440-adb8-517c3ebdc0c0?syn-25a6b1a6=1 "") with other health insurance stocks in January after the government proposed increasing certain 2027 payments to companies that provide coverage for seniors and people with disabilities by just 0.09 per cent.

The stocks rallied earlier this month when the administration reversed its stance and approved higher increases for [Medicare payments to insurance companies](https://www.ft.com/content/1683cb40-51af-485d-b05c-84f60f33b1bb?FTCamp=engage%2FCAPI%2Fwebsite%2FChannel_muckrack%2F%2FB2B&syn-25a6b1a6=1 "").

UnitedHealth’s shares sank last year after its previous chief executive Andrew Witty stepped down amid slumping earnings. It brought back former chief executive Stephen Hemsley to run the company.

UnitedHealth said it was still facing trouble at its Optum unit, which runs medical practices. Optum’s earnings dropped to $3.3bn in the first quarter from $3.9bn a year ago.

At the company’s healthcare division, earnings rose to $5.7bn in the quarter from $5.2bn last year. UnitedHealth has increased some insurance prices and also dropped 965,000 patients from Medicare Advantage, which are government-subsidised insurance plans popular with older Americans.

UnitedHealth also said it would buy back $2bn of its stock by the end of June. The shares have fallen 2 per cent so far this year and are down 24 per cent over the past 12 months.

Marion Maréchal, a member of the Le Pen family, has pitched a broad rightwing alliance as the only way for Rassemblement National to win the next French presidential election.

The 36-year-old niece of three-time presidential candidate Marine Le Pen has made a political comeback as she seeks a central role in the campaign to succeed Emmanuel Macron in 2027.

[Maréchal](https://www.ft.com/stream/006e1366-d55d-47d0-8bde-bc52691421f9 "") told the FT that the far right in France should emulate how Giorgia Meloni rose to power in Italy by uniting the far right and traditional right. “This is the path that has enabled the nationalist right in Europe and elsewhere to take part in governments or even to lead them,” she said.

Maréchal espouses more socially and economically conservative views than the RN, unabashedly promoting Catholicism, warning about the dangers of what she calls France’s “Islamification” and the ‘‘woke agenda” on transgender rights and anti-racism. Critics accuse her of xenophobia and of attempts to undermine France’s secularism.

“I carry a complementary voice to the RN that can bring in a different set of voters. It includes being more engaged in protecting European civilisation and its Christian roots, more liberal on the economy and more conservative on social issues,” she said.

\[DRAFT 1\] French politician popularity27

04020François HollandeRaphaël GlucksmannBruno RetailleauGabriel AttalGérald DarmaninÉric CiottiÉdouard PhilippeMarion MaréchalMarine Le PenJordan Bardella4343414135353434313131312929292927272727

Maréchal enjoys a popularity rate of up to 35 per cent, behind her aunt or RN party leader Jordan Bardella, but ahead of many established politicians vying for the presidency.

She stopped using the Le Pen name in 2017 when she stepped back from politics. In 2022, she defected to a rival far-right party led by anti-Islam firebrand Éric Zemmour. That led to a two-year rift with Marine Le Pen, with the pair only reconciling when Maréchal broke with Zemmour.

Her small Identité-Libertés (IDL) party is now allied with the RN, even as she continues to promote ideas that are taboo for her aunt.

With the publication of an autobiography titled _If you feel like a Le Pen_ in January, Maréchal has sought to reassert her role in the family dynasty that for more than 50 years has been synonymous with the French far right. Her late grandfather, Jean-Marie Le Pen, who founded the Front National in the 1970s, used the title phrase in a letter to urge her to run for office at the age of 22.

In the book, Maréchal casts herself as the guardian of the Le Pen family ethos of “refusing the politically correct, or easy conventions” and standing for the French people against the elite.

![Several copies of Marion Maréchal’s book “Si tu te sens Le Pen” displayed on a table at a book signing event, with people gathered behind.](https://images.ft.com/v3/image/raw/https%3A%2F%2Fd1e00ek4ebabms.cloudfront.net%2Fproduction%2F7e8a017d-8257-4505-80cd-5defffedebae.jpg?source=next-article&fit=scale-down&quality=highest&width=700&dpr=1)With the publication of an autobiography titled ‘If you feel like a Le Pen’, Maréchal has sought to reassert her role in the family dynasty© Roland Macri/Hans Lucas/Reuters

Her message holds particular significance now since Marine Le Pen, who excluded her father from the party and rebranded it as RN as part of an effort to “detoxify” it from his antisemitic legacy, now faces a ban to stand for public office in an EU embezzlement case.

Le Pen has already said Bardella would run in her place if she loses an appeal in July. Polls now show either Le Pen or Bardella would make the run-off with about 30 per cent of the vote, far ahead of other contenders.

But her exclusion would be a political earthquake because the Le Pen family for the first time in decades would no longer field a candidate for the presidency.

Although Maréchal does not plan to run herself, she said she would throw herself into the 2027 campaign to try to forge a “union of the right”.

“I am ready to go to battle because I have two young daughters, and every day that goes by, I can see my country decline,” she said.

“We could, tomorrow, become a majority-Muslim country. And I know that such a country would no longer be France as we have known it, nor one where my daughters can be free French women. I feel a sense of urgency.”

![Marion Maréchal sitting at a table with hands clasped, wearing a dark blazer and white blouse, looking towards the camera.](https://images.ft.com/v3/image/raw/https%3A%2F%2Fd1e00ek4ebabms.cloudfront.net%2Fproduction%2F77750148-2546-46f7-9d9e-4ae5c81188eb.jpg?source=next-article&fit=scale-down&quality=highest&width=700&dpr=1)Maréchal: ‘The vast majority of rightwing voters, regardless of which party they back, support unifying on the right’© Magali Delporte/FT

Elected as a member of the European parliament in 2024, she sits in the same group with Meloni’s MEPs, a position that Maréchal said allows her to avoid the so-called cordon sanitaire that excludes the RN from positions of power within the assembly. Her husband Vincenzo Sofo is an MEP for Meloni’s Brothers of Italy party, so the couple have known the Italian premier for years.

Maréchal says she has built bridges to traditional rightwing parties in the EU assembly, including the LR from France. “It allows us to be a pivot between the RN and the LR which is similar to the role I can play on the national level,” she said.

Encouraged by her aunt to get to know Bardella, Maréchal now meets with him regularly to talk strategy and to discuss the RN’s preparations for the 2027 race. The party is working on a new platform, which is expected to include strict immigration controls, a crime crackdown and cutting France’s contribution to the EU budget.

Le Pen and Bardella have also picked up their networking with business leaders. Le Pen recently dined with a small group of CEOs including Bernard Arnault andTotalEnergies boss Patrick Pouyanné — a shift from the past when many were unwilling to meet the RN.

Le Pen has long rejected relying solely on a rightwing union to win power, saying that she wants to appeal to “all the French”, including her long-held base of working-class and low-income voters, many of whom used to vote for the left.

Maréchal acknowledges the differences, but points to Le Pen’s successful alliance with Éric Ciotti, who left the LR in 2024 to ally with the RN, helping him last month win the mayorship in Nice, France’s fifth-largest city.

“The vast majority of rightwing voters, regardless of which party they back, support unifying on the right,” she said. “There is nothing on a policy level which is irreconcilable between us that could not be handled within a coalition.”

Maréchal said she could negotiate a joint platform with Le Pen, Bardella, Ciotti and LR leader Bruno Retailleau.

“What is sure is that \[on the economy\] the RN is a party that is undeniably more interventionist, more state-driven, and more redistributive than I am, but we also agree on many things,” Maréchal said.

_Data visualisation by Janina Conboye_

This article is an on-site version of our Chris Giles on Central Banks newsletter. Premium subscribers can sign up [here](https://ep.ft.com/newsletters/subscribe?newsletterIds=6501cc9ec6e3c91c18b0b9e6 "") to get the newsletter delivered every Tuesday. Standard subscribers can upgrade to Premium [here](https://www.ft.com/manage/subscription/change/713f1e28-0bc5-8261-f1e6-eebab6f7600e?segmentId=5d1c2689-3304-f81f-a9e5-b3e96e93c176 ""), or [explore](https://www.ft.com/newsletters "") all FT newsletters

Kevin Warsh will make the case in front of the Senate Committee on Banking, Housing and Urban Affairs today that he is the right person to become US Federal Reserve chair. But there are many hurdles he needs to clear before he takes the most important job in central banking.

Will Warsh’s [undisclosed financial investments](https://www.ft.com/content/ba9a86fe-bc92-4146-ba07-c724578deb14?syn-25a6b1a6=1 "") prove an obstacle? Will Republican senator Thom Tillis continue to block Warsh until the criminal probe into Fed chair Jay Powell is dropped? Will the Department of Justice drop that probe to smooth the path for Warsh? Will President [Donald Trump fire Powell](https://www.ft.com/content/74acb3fe-648d-4d2f-bc1d-1b942b47020f "") if he remains chair after his term ends on May 15? How quickly will a judge throw out any such attempt? Will the president seek to nominate a different chair in the interim? Will the rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee keep Powell as chair even if Trump nominates a new temporary one?

I am not going to attempt to answer any of these questions because, frankly, I don’t know what will happen. But I am going to make one assertion: ultimately, these questions will prove transitory. I want to focus on more substantive points regarding the nomination, which I hope the committee will probe.

I have been delving into Warsh’s past views for benchmarks against which we can judge positions he will lay out in his hearing. All of my research tallies with [Warsh’s opening statement](https://www.ft.com/content/3654184a-4ffc-44d9-9d21-61229bf9fbd7?syn-25a6b1a6=1 ""), which came out last night. Consistency is thus something the Fed chair nominee clearly values. For Warsh’s views as a Fed governor between 2006 and 2011, I have made liberal use of a [helpful AI tool](https://x.com/FOMCInsight ""), which limits its search domain to Fed archives.

### Will Warsh be Trump’s lackey?

In the past, Warsh has had a sophisticated, consistent view on central bank independence. He has made the case that independence is a wise outcome of the political process, but that central bankers need to reciprocate with good economic outcomes. They should not be “ [treated as pampered princes](https://www.hoover.org/sites/default/files/research/docs/Commanding%20Heights%20April%2025%202025%20DC.pdf "")”, nor see independence as “ [some inalienable right, some entitlement](https://fraser.stlouisfed.org/files/docs/historical/federal%20reserve%20history/bog_members_statements/warsh_20100326.pdf "")”. Rather, it is “a revocable privilege”.

Giving [evidence to a UK House of Lords committee in 2023](https://committees.parliament.uk/oralevidence/13561/html/ ""), he said: “Independence granted by government demands a few other things. It demands that the central bank also acts independently without favour to any one side.”

There will naturally be some scepticism about Warsh on this front — not because he is a Republican but because he was highly partisan when angling for the nomination. In November last year, he [wrote](https://www.wsj.com/opinion/the-federal-reserves-broken-leadership-43629c87 ""): “The most underappreciated characteristic of the Trump administration is its admiration for individual achievement. Treating people based on their merits rather than their status or sensibilities is the renewed American credo.” This does not sit well with the [evidence of Trump’s transactional capitalism](https://www.ft.com/content/2a7f4575-8c94-4258-9ff4-6b6c74815d07?syn-25a6b1a6=1 ""), which favours loyalists.

The hearing will be an opportunity for Warsh to demonstrate some distance between himself and the administration.

### Does an energy shock require tighter monetary policy?

In 2011, Warsh’s [answer was broadly “yes”](https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/files/FOMC20101103meeting.pdf ""). At a November 2011 FOMC meeting, he questioned whether staff forecasts in the Fed’s “Tealbook” were right to assume commodity prices would fall in line with futures pricing. He was also “less certain” than the staff that “movements in the prices of energy and imports would only have minor implications for domestic inflation”. Instead, higher commodity prices would “find their way into sales prices”, he said, with loose monetary policy encouraging higher inflation expectations. His views 15 years ago were similar to those [expressed by Fed governor Christopher Waller](https://www.ft.com/content/5a936d43-b46a-423d-9095-ec1cbd74895f?syn-25a6b1a6=1 "") on Friday.

Although he disagreed with the chair and the rest of the committee on their monetary policy stance at the 2011 meeting, he did not formally dissent. The question today is whether he has changed his mind about energy shocks and, if so, why.

### Where should interest rates go?

Trump [said last week](https://www.foxbusiness.com/media/trump-threatens-fire-powell-blasts-fed-leadership-incompetent "") (11 mins) that interest rates would fall “when Kevin gets in”, despite Treasury secretary Scott Bessent [saying](https://www.semafor.com/article/04/13/2026/treasury-secretary-scott-bessent-us-should-wait-and-see-before-lowering-interest-rates "") the central bank was “doing the right thing by sitting and watching”.

Warsh made clear his view that [lower rates were needed](https://www.wsj.com/opinion/the-federal-reserves-broken-leadership-43629c87 "") last November. But this came as part of a package alongside tighter financial conditions for Wall Street, so was more of a redistribution of the effects of monetary policy rather than a simple loosening.

He also said a coming productivity boom would be a “significant disinflationary force”, implying a bias towards easier policy based on the conviction that AI would lower inflationary pressures.

We need to know why he disagrees with the many economists, [including Fed officials](https://www.ft.com/content/7fa0a049-9d65-41ad-83e3-1f38fd8938fd?syn-25a6b1a6=1 ""), who think a productivity boom implies structurally higher interest rates to generate the additional savings needed to balance the AI investment surge.

### What is Warsh’s theory of inflation?

Warsh is fond of saying “ [inflation is a choice](https://committees.parliament.uk/oralevidence/13561/html/ "")”, which occurs when the US government “ [spends too much and prints too much](https://www.wsj.com/opinion/the-federal-reserves-broken-leadership-43629c87 "")” and which the central bank allows to happen. Questions regarding the Trump administration’s fiscal loosening and high budget deficits will, therefore, be interesting.

It is also true that Warsh has held [multiple theories of inflation](https://www.hoover.org/sites/default/files/research/docs/Commanding%20Heights%20April%2025%202025%20DC.pdf ""). He thinks the Fed erred in thinking that having a price stability objective was sufficient to make low inflation a reality. He also thinks it believed its models too much and that it allowed excessive money creation. These are compatible with Warsh’s core belief that government spending and money-printing is the root of inflation. But it will be interesting to know how he links them in a coherent theory.

### What does Warsh really think about the Fed’s balance sheet?

Warsh has long opposed the rise in the Fed’s now [$6.7tn](https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/bst_recenttrends.htm "") balance sheet, which [he blames](https://www.hoover.org/sites/default/files/research/docs/Commanding%20Heights%20April%2025%202025%20DC.pdf "") for fostering inflation, blurring the boundaries between fiscal and monetary policy and undermining central bank independence.

These are the things we want to know: how quickly does he want to move from a rising to a shrinking balance sheet? Is changing banking regulation so financial institutions want to hold US government bonds rather than reserves at the Fed the primary lever he will pull? What difference would that make? If the private sector were encouraged to buy more government bonds, would that require higher yields? If interest rates were cut in return, who are the winners and losers?

This is perhaps the policy that will define the Warsh Fed.

### Will Warsh seek deference from other Fed officials?

Before he quit as a Fed governor in 2011, Warsh made clear he disagreed with the central bank’s policy. But he did not dissent against the then chair, Ben Bernanke. The [transcript of the November 2010](https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/files/FOMC20101103meeting.pdf "") FOMC meeting shows Warsh telling Bernanke that he was leading the committee in the wrong direction, but that “this is called the Bernanke Fed for a reason” and “I wouldn’t want to undermine at this important moment the chance that this \[QE\] program could be successful”.

Warsh [in 2023 also noted](https://committees.parliament.uk/oralevidence/13561/html/ "") that “the chairman at the Federal Reserve has an inordinate say over the final votes”. The question, then, is whether he will demand the deference of others on the FOMC that he showed Bernanke.

### Warsh’s attitude to theory, conviction and data

Warsh has long been an economist [strong on conviction](https://www.ft.com/content/167c5c6b-97de-41da-911f-4f4f8193e620?syn-25a6b1a6=1 ""), as is clear from his views on government spending and money-printing. Yet he has generally been sceptical in the past of being data-dependent. The problem with data dependence, he told UK lords in 2023, is that “the data is not very good” and “dependence says ‘we’re going to make policy based on old data’”.

One thing is certain: Warsh wants the Fed to [look at more real-time information](https://www.ft.com/content/a11d75a8-5885-4d65-97c1-0cab85f7df4d?syn-25a6b1a6=1 ""). But this raises questions about how he wants to use these rather noisy new datasets and what the movements that would force a change of view would be.

### Warsh and the relationship between the Fed and US Treasury

In something that surprises many Americans, Warsh has long seen little problem in the US Treasury having a say on monetary policy. “The Fed is not independent from government. It is independent within government,” he said in [a 2010 speech.](https://fraser.stlouisfed.org/files/docs/historical/federal%20reserve%20history/bog_members_statements/warsh_20100326.pdf "")

His view is essentially the one adopted in Britain, where a UK Treasury representative attends meetings of the Monetary Policy Committee and the Bank of England only has operational independence from government for certain prescribed roles. In 2023, Warsh said this of the UK: “My bias was, ‘boy, that must be quite the intrusion. The Treasury secretary doesn’t sit at the Federal Reserve’, but I was surprised and delighted at how well the culture works.”

What is not known is whether he just admires the UK from afar or wants to give the US executive a greater say.

### Warsh on financial regulation, crypto and stablecoins

One area Warsh is clear the Fed should not be independent is in financial regulation. “I do not believe the Fed is owed any particular deference in bank regulatory and supervisory policy,” [he said last year](https://www.hoover.org/sites/default/files/research/docs/Commanding%20Heights%20April%2025%202025%20DC.pdf "").

This is a broadly principled position. In fact, BoE governor Andrew Bailey last week made [similar arguments](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/speech/2026/april/andrew-bailey-panellist-at-columbia-university-new-york ""). But it does raise the question of how the Fed will view financial stability risk from banks, crypto and stablecoins when the administration is in favour of deregulation.

### Warsh on communications

Warsh has made clear his distaste for a cacophony of voices on monetary policy. “I would say that communications are important, but we should not be talking so much,” [he said to the UK parliament](https://committees.parliament.uk/oralevidence/13561/html/ ""). In the same hearing, he advocated fewer monetary policy meetings.

“Once policymakers reveal their economic forecast, they can become prisoners of their own words,” [he later said in 2025](https://www.hoover.org/sites/default/files/research/docs/Commanding%20Heights%20April%2025%202025%20DC.pdf ""), which explains his dislike of the FOMC’s dot plots. The questions that matter here are how quickly Warsh would like to bring in reforms and what they would entail.

## What I’ve been reading and watching

- If you want a great primer about stablecoins, which recognises costs and benefits and avoids tech bro jargon, read [the speech](https://www.bis.org/speeches/sp260420.htm "") this week by the Bank for International Settlements general manager, Pablo Hernández de Cos.

- The greatest threat to the global economy would come from the Iranian regime realising the Strait of Hormuz is more powerful than a nuclear weapon or missiles. Our reporting from Tehran suggests [this is exactly the lesson](https://www.ft.com/content/f3c8decc-0c59-49fa-9854-ddd9103279f4?syn-25a6b1a6=1 "") being learnt.

- At Monetary Policy Radar, we have developed a new real-time measure of household inflation expectations from posts on X. The good news is that they [moderated a bit in April](https://www.ft.com/content/b23a220f-9b39-4a22-b27e-6fdc5f4b79f3 "").

- Did you know the European Central Bank has its own art collection? That is the question [it asked on social media](https://twitter.com/ecb/status/2045065961124803005 "") last week. If you are in Frankfurt on April 25, you can see it as long as you register before Wednesday.


## One last chart

Last week, the IMF urged countries not to subsidise fuel or lower energy taxes that made adjustment to the Iran supply shock more difficult. Many governments simply ignored it, as the chart below shows. These countries include those that have made a virtue of international co-ordination, such as Canada, where Prime Minister Mark Carney [announced the suspension of fuel duties](https://x.com/MarkJCarney/status/2044435776276947155?s=20 "") until September.

# Global clean power growth points to permanent shift, analysts say

Evidence grows of structural move away from fossil fuels to meet rising electricity demand

Global clean power output grew faster than electricity demand last year, according to new figures which analysts argue represent a structural shift away from fossil fuels that will persist beyond the Middle East war.

The rapid growth of solar power in particular contributed to a small annual decrease in output from fossil fuel power stations, according to the analysis by independent think-tank Ember.

It marked the first time fossil fuel output had fallen since 2020 pandemic year, Ember said, highlighting the enormous impact that solar and wind power are having on electricity systems. The report also covered clean energy sources including nuclear, geothermal and biofuels.

Renewable energy sources also generated more electricity than coal-fired power stations in 2025, Ember said, “for the first time in the modern power system”, extending a trend first seen in the first half of last year.

The ability of renewable sources to meet electricity demand growth, if sustained, is significant because it suggests they are on course to eat into fossil-fuel supplies rather than just add to them.

Aditya Lolla, interim managing director at Ember, said low-carbon electricity was “scaling fast enough to absorb rising global electricity demand” and its momentum was now a “structural reality”.

The review by Ember, which has received funding from groups including the European Climate Foundation and the Sequoia Climate Foundation, comes as the Iran war is causing several major economies to lean on coal-fired power as a replacement for gas-fired power because of high gas prices.

However, energy industry sources and analysts expect the war will also accelerate the rollout of wind and solar power, as countries try to insulate themselves from fossil fuel supply shocks.

Separate data analysed by Ember show that China’s exports of solar panels, batteries and electric cars reached a record high of $21.9bn in the month of March 2026, which was 70 per cent higher than in March the previous year.

Export levels have been influenced by tax changes set to come into effect in April, but highlight huge demand for green technology as well as China’s role supplying the world with green energy equipment while the US’s war against Iran disrupts fossil fuel supplies.

![Rows of solar panels cover a tidal flat as a crane on a barge installs additional panels at the CNNC Tianwan power plant.](https://images.ft.com/v3/image/raw/https%3A%2F%2Fd1e00ek4ebabms.cloudfront.net%2Fproduction%2F05583675-8961-4374-baf6-8fe7bb574a17.jpg?source=next-article&fit=scale-down&quality=highest&width=700&dpr=1)Solar panel installation on the CNNC Tianwan tidal flat photovoltaic power plant in Lianyungang, in China’s eastern Jiangsu province.© AFP via Getty Images

China’s exports of solar, lithium-ion batteries and electric vehicles have totalled $200bn over the past 12 months, according to Ember’s data, or about 6 per cent of all its exports.

Wind and solar farms were installed at pace globally last year: roughly 647 gigawatts of solar power were added, alongside a record 165GW of wind, according to figures published this week by the Global Wind Energy Council. This is sufficient to power the equivalent of 200mn average western homes.

In terms of output, global solar power generation across the year increased by 636 terawatt-hours — more than double the UK’s annual consumption — wind by 205 TWh; nuclear by 35 TWh; and other low carbon generation by 11 TWh, according to Ember.

That was greater than the 849 TWh increase in global electricity demand, while generation from fossil fuel power stations fell by 38 TWh.

The report also highlights the growing role of batteries to store solar and wind output and discharge it when needed, as falling costs turn the technology into “core infrastructure”.

However, for now fossil fuel generators are still needed to meet the night-time demand peak, and their night-time output grew.

“While solar meets daytime demand, fossil generation keeps growing in the dark hours,” Ember said.

“In most countries, demand rose across all hours, so even when solar met the majority or all demand growth on an annual basis, fossil generation often continued to rise outside daylight hours, as evident in countries like India and the US.”

_The writer is a researcher at the Oxford Sustainable Finance Group_

The US cattle and beef industry is in a bad way. The megadrought currently plaguing the western states has been going on for 22 years, with no signs of abating. The quality of grazing land has declined, raising feed costs and driving the cattle herd to its lowest numbers since 1951. Meatpackers are paying high prices for live cattle, which would normally cause ranchers to increase herd sizes — but that’s not happening. Costs are passed on to consumers, but meatpacking plants are also closing.

Despite all this turmoil, there has been a stubborn refusal within the industry to acknowledge that it is the source of its own destruction. The longer this goes on, the greater the costs will be for everyone involved.

We are in this situation for two reasons: rising temperatures and groundwater depletion. Rising temperatures are the result of greenhouse gas emissions — a not insubstantial amount of which comes from cattle. Methane emitted from cattle has roughly 80 times the warming potential of CO₂ over a 20-year period.

It is true that livestock production makes up a relatively small portion of greenhouse gases globally — somewhere between 12-20 per cent. It is also true that US cattle contribute only a small portion to total emissions from agriculture, approximately 3.8 per cent. However, even with its shrinking numbers, the US still has the third largest herd size in the world after Brazil and India. Furthermore, the fact that methane has a much shorter lifespan than CO₂ (12 years vs hundreds) means it is critical in limiting global warming in the near term — putting cattle front and centre.

There is no silver bullet. Feed additives are only applicable while cattle are in a feedlot as opposed to grazing, and questions remain on their economic viability. Reports that the industry could [reduce emissions by 30 per cent](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43016-024-01031-9 "") are based largely on sequestration rather than direct methane abatement. In short, the beef industry cannot be fully decoupled from its emissions.

Next, the [long-running drought](https://www.ft.com/content/0467d3a7-0fa9-4895-8feb-6be4343e01be?syn-25a6b1a6=1 "") is exacerbated by the perpetual over-withdrawal of water from the Colorado River and the Ogallala Aquifer. Most of this goes to producing animal feed, with dairy and beef several times more water intensive than other sources of protein.

The reality is that the cattle industry is eating its own tail — using up the very resources it needs to sustain itself. It is a clear example of “double materiality” — whereby an industry is the victim of the physical risks it creates for itself. With warming and water depletion rates on their current trajectory, it will not be able to recover its former scale. Instead it will continue to phase down with more and more [stranded assets](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43016-025-01170-7 "") including machinery, meatpacking plants and, if water levels can no longer sustain agriculture or development, land.

Reality has struck and its financial implications are both unavoidable and unattractive. There are two options from here. The first is that the cattle industry, policymakers and investors wait for the inevitable to be forced upon them. Or, more proactively, the industry could be supported through a well-planned transition process, in which both government and lenders make a concerted effort to support cattle ranchers in moving to alternative livelihoods.

More ranchers than we think may be ready. One farmer in Oklahoma told me he wanted to get out of cattle and plant stone fruit trees on his land but struggled to get a loan because it would take too long to generate cash flows. Creative [transition finance](https://www.ft.com/content/f2a6149f-4bdc-43c3-80ae-c9d46b9b4486 "") mechanisms will be critical in supporting ranches through this process. [Direct payments for retirement](https://theconversation.com/coal-power-plants-were-paid-to-close-is-it-time-to-do-the-same-for-slaughterhouses-257418 "") may also need to be considered.

The alternative is for the cattle industry to continue ignoring the climate risks already hurting it and be left with nothing at the end of it. This is a problem of our own making. Only by our own hands can we circumvent it.

MSCI has kicked out two Indonesian tycoon-owned stocks from its indices but delayed a decision on whether to downgrade south-east Asia’s largest economy, adding to uncertainty after the global index provider’s warning triggered the country’s worst stock rout in years.

MSCI said in a statement late on Monday it would “delete securities” flagged by Indonesian authorities as having high shareholding concentrations.

Although it did not identify any companies, Indonesia’s stock exchange this month named Barito Renewables and Dian Swastatika Sentosa as among nine companies with tight ownership. Only those two stocks were part of MSCI’s Indonesia indices.

Barito’s shares fell 7 per cent on Tuesday, while DSS tumbled 13 per cent. The broader Jakarta Composite index shed 0.6 per cent.

The index provider also said it was reviewing measures announced by [Indonesia](https://www.ft.com/indonesia "") to address concerns over concentrated ownership and a lack of transparency. MSCI said it would provide an update on the market’s status in June, later than the initial deadline of May.

The concerns had led MSCI to warn in January that it could downgrade Indonesia from an emerging to frontier market, [triggering a sharp sell-off](https://www.ft.com/content/e96efe7b-5fd4-4a66-a898-b529b23e264 ""). Jakarta’s main stock index has lost nearly $190bn in value since the warning, making it one of the worst-performing equity markets.

Gary Tan, an emerging markets portfolio manager at Allspring Global Investments, said MSCI’s latest move reduced the risk of a downgrade and the “extension to June suggests Indonesia is broadly moving in the right direction”.

MSCI had taken “the more prudent step of removing stocks flagged under the new high shareholding concentration framework rather than making a broader index call”, he said.

In response to MSCI’s warning in January, Indonesian authorities announced several reforms, including doubling the minimum free float, the portion of stocks available to public markets, to 15 per cent, though it has given companies three years to comply.

Indonesia’s exchange also tightened regulations on disclosures, requiring shareholders to report ownership above 1 per cent, stricter than the previous 5 per cent, and identified companies with high shareholding concentrations.

The exchange said a limited number of shareholders controlled 97.3 per cent of Barito, a geothermal energy producer owned by Prajogo Pangestu, Indonesia’s richest person, and 95.76 per cent of DSS, a coal miner owned by the Widjaja family as part of its Sinar Mas conglomerate.

OJK, Indonesia’s financial regulator, has said it is investigating a broad range of potential misconduct, including “market manipulation and other trading practices intended to create a false or misleading appearance of trading activity”.

Regulators and investors allege that in some cases, insiders hold shares through nominees and trade with each other, creating the impression of liquidity and moving share prices.

Tycoon-owned conglomerates have been flagged by global investors as among the most problematic, with wild price swings and high valuations that investors say are not justified.

Barito told the FT that “decisions made by index providers are outside of our control”, adding that the company had fully complied with disclosure and free-float requirements and that regulators had not indicated the company violated any capital markets regulations.

DSS, Indonesia’s stock exchange and OJK did not respond to requests for comment.

_Additional reporting by Diana Mariska in Jakarta_

# Smartphones to be banned in schools in England

Government bows to pressure from teachers, parents and the Conservative Party

Sir Keir Starmer has announced that all schools in England will be ordered to ban smartphones after the prime minister bowed to pressure from the Conservative Party, teachers and parents.

Starmer had previously resisted calls for a ban, saying that most [schools](https://www.ft.com/uk-schools "") already had restrictions in place via “non-statutory guidance” to headteachers to keep schools phone free.

Labour announced the policy change in the House of Lords on Monday night as peers gathered to vote on an amendment to the schools bill from Conservative peer Baroness Barran.

Her amendment, passed by a majority of 107, would have banned children from having smartphones at school. The Lords [had already voted in February](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/c5y3npyjyl7o "") for a school phones ban.

If the government had not announced the U-turn, the schools bill could have faced further delays in parliament.

Baroness Smith of Malvern, skills minister, said the government’s amendment would “create a clear legal requirement that the guidance must be followed unless there is a legally justifiable reason for schools not to do so”.

She said: “We’ve listened to concerns about how we support headteachers in delivering on this policy and we have listened to parliament.”

It comes as a ban on children born after 2008 from ever being able to buy cigarettes has been agreed by parliament. The final draft of the tobacco and vapes bill cleared the House of Lords on Monday and will now go for royal assent. The ban was first proposed by Rishi Sunak’s Conservative government in 2023.

Bridget Phillipson, education secretary, wrote to schools earlier this year encouraging them to follow new guidance saying that schools should be phone free all day.

The education department said that guidance would now be put on a statutory footing. “Mobile phones have no place in schools and the majority already prohibit them. This amendment makes existing guidance statutory, giving legal force to what schools are already doing in practice.”

Conservative shadow education secretary Laura Trott said the amendment was “fantastic news for headteachers, parents and pupils across the country”.

“For over a year, Labour dismissed this as an unnecessary gimmick, and just last week the education minister claimed the problem had already been solved,” Trott wrote on X. “I’m glad they’ve now listened, this is the right step for improving behaviour and raising attainment in our classrooms.”

Pepe Di’Iasio, general secretary of the Association of School and College Leaders, said the statutory ban would not change much, given most pupils were already not allowed to use phones in classes.

“What would really be helpful is for the government to make funding available to schools for the safe and secure storage of mobile phones, such as storage lockers or locked pouches.”

Only 5 per cent of parents believe students should be allowed to use their phones during lessons, according to new polling from Opinium.

Research by the children’s commissioner for England last year showed that 99.8 per cent of primary schools and 90 per cent of secondary schools already restricted the use of phones in schools.

The UK government is still consulting on whether to impose an Australia-style ban on [social media for under-16s](https://www.ft.com/content/482a3067-602e-4111-931c-eaa8b1edc8dc?syn-25a6b1a6=1 ""). Starmer last week told tech bosses that while he believed social media could be made safer for British children, curbs on access would be “preferable to a world where harm is the price of participation”.
